data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse resulted from a synchronized climatic shock (the 3.2ka event) that specifically targeted the rain-fed agricultural hinterlands of the palace economies.", "mechanism": "A multi-decadal mega-drought reduced agricultural yields below the threshold required to support the hyper-specialized, non-food-producing palace elites and standing armies, triggering internal revolt and mass migration.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data from Eastern Mediterranean sediment cores showing stable precipitation levels between 1200-1150 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Relies on the 3.2ka BP aridification event; explains Egypt's survival via its reliance on Nile irrigation rather than rainfall."}
